title: Flex alignment and order

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Flex alignment and order; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Flex alignment and order
sources.claim: Flex sizing combines basis, grow, shrink, intrinsic minimums, gaps, and alignment to distribute available or negative space.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Flex alignment and order
sources.claim: Flex sizing combines basis, grow, shrink, intrinsic minimums, gaps, and alignment to distribute available or negative space.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Flex sizing combines basis, grow, shrink, intrinsic minimums, gaps, and alignment to distribute available or negative space.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A text item gets min-width:0 so it can shrink beside a fixed icon without overflowing. Your goal is to debug alignment and verify meaningful source and focus order. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Normal flow and display; Intrinsic sizing and constraints; Flex axes and sizing; html navigation skip. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Flex sizing combines basis, grow, shrink, intrinsic minimums, gaps, and alignment to distribute available or negative space. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: The default min-size can prevent shrinking, auto margins absorb space, and align-content affects lines rather than a single line. This draft therefore rejects: flex:1 guarantees equal final widths regardless of content and minimum size.

includes: Align items and groups while avoiding visual order that contradicts DOM and keyboard order.
Debug alignment and verify meaningful source and focus order.
excludes: The default min-size can prevent shrinking, auto margins absorb space, and align-content affects lines rather than a single line.

outcomes: Debug alignment and verify meaningful source and focus order.

examples.title: Flex alignment and order: concrete case
examples.situation: A text item gets min-width:0 so it can shrink beside a fixed icon without overflowing.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A text item gets min-width:0 so it can shrink beside a fixed icon without overflowing.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Account for basis and intrinsic minimums; flex growth alone does not guarantee equal final sizes.
examples.result: The result follows because Account for basis and intrinsic minimums; flex growth alone does not guarantee equal final sizes.

examples.title: Flex alignment and order: boundary case
examples.situation: The default min-size can prevent shrinking, auto margins absorb space, and align-content affects lines rather than a single line.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: The default min-size can prevent shrinking, auto margins absorb space, and align-content affects lines rather than a single line.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: flex:1 guarantees equal final widths regardless of content and minimum size.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Account for basis and intrinsic minimums; flex growth alone does not guarantee equal final sizes.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: flex:1 guarantees equal final widths regardless of content and minimum size.
misconceptions.correction: Account for basis and intrinsic minimums; flex growth alone does not guarantee equal final sizes.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: flex:1 guarantees equal final widths regardless of content and minimum size.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Flex alignment and order, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Flex alignment and order before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
